text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
39-year-old premenopausal female with stage IIB (ypT1b ypN1 M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier. She completed neoadjuvant Keytruda in combination with dose-dense Adriamycin/cyclo (four cycles) followed by weekly pacli (twelve weeks, with Keytruda), and subsequently underwent bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection. Final pathology demonstrated non-pathologic complete response (RCB class II). She is currently mid-course through adjuvant cape and pembro maintenance. This study is obtained as routine surveillance approximately six months post-surgery to evaluate for locoregional recurrence and occult distant metastatic disease.

COMPARISON:
Prior staging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed at the time of initial diagnosis (approximately 14 months prior).

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast material. Images were acquired during the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, and a chest acquisition was obtained in the arterial/portal phase. Reconstructions performed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at standard and thin-slice intervals. No oral contrast was administered.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Breast/Chest Wall:
The patient has undergone bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy. Bilateral smooth, ovoid subpectoral soft tissue density structures are present bilaterally, consistent with bilateral tissue expanders in situ. A small linear hyperdense focus within the left-sided expander likely represents the injection port marker. The overlying skin envelope is smooth and of uniform thickness bilaterally, with no focal skin thickening, retraction, or subcutaneous nodularity to suggest local recurrence or skin infiltration. The pectoral musculature appears intact and symmetric bilaterally. No abnormal soft tissue mass or nodularity is identified along either chest wall.

Left Axilla:
The left axillary region demonstrates expected post-surgical change. There is mild stranding of the axillary fat consistent with prior lymph node dissection. No discrete enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the left axilla. The surgical bed is without focal fluid collection, hematoma, or evidence of nodal recurrence. The largest residual lymph node-like structure in the left axilla measures approximately 7 mm in short axis, with a maintained fatty hilum, and is not morphologically suspicious.

Right Axilla:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are seen in the right axilla. The right axillary fat is preserved.

Lungs:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations are identified. No ground-glass opacities are seen, and specifically there is no residual or new bilateral parenchymal opacity to suggest immune-mediated or drug-related pneumonitis. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent through the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinal contour is unremarkable. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. The heart is normal in size. Pericardium appears intact without effusion. The thoracic aorta and great vessels demonstrate normal caliber and contour for patient age. No mediastinal or hilar lymph node enlargement. Subcarinal, paratracheal, and prevascular lymph nodes are all within normal size limits (none exceeding 8 mm short axis). No internal mammary chain adenopathy.

Skeletal Structures (Thorax):
The visualized thoracic spine, ribs, and clavicles demonstrate preserved cortical bone without lytic or blastic lesions. No focal destructive bony abnormality. No pathologic fracture. Bone density appears appropriate for patient age.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous parenchymal attenuation throughout without focal hypodense or hyperdense lesions. Enhancement pattern following IV contrast is uniform and appropriate. No hepatic masses identified. The hepatic veins and portal veins are patent. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable; no gallstones. Common bile duct is not dilated. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size (approximately 9.5 cm in craniocaudal dimension) and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is of normal morphology and size with a smooth contour. No focal pancreatic mass or ductal dilatation. The peripancreatic fat planes are preserved.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands are slender and uninodular in configuration. No adrenal mass, adenoma, or thickening. Adrenal size and morphology are within normal limits bilaterally.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and normally. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No perirenal lymphadenopathy. Bilateral renal cortical thickness is maintained without focal cortical defects. The ureters are not dilated to the extent of visualization.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes:
No enlarged retroperitoneal, para-aortic, aortocaval, or mesenteric lymph nodes are identified. No soft tissue mass in the retroperitoneum. The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber.

Bowel:
Small bowel loops and the large bowel are of normal caliber without bowel wall thickening, mucosal abnormality, or obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites.

Skeletal Structures (Abdomen):
The visualized lumbar spine and pelvic bony structures appear intact. No focal lytic or sclerotic lesions. Vertebral body heights maintained.

---

PELVIS:

Uterus and Adnexa:
The uterus is present and of normal size with a smooth outer contour. The uterine fundus measures approximately 7.5 cm in length. No focal uterine mass. The bilateral adnexal regions are unremarkable. No discrete ovarian masses or cysts are identified. No free pelvic fluid or ascites. Note: The patient is premenopausal with intact uterus; bilateral ovaries appear normal in size and morphology, without suspicious solid component or abnormal vascularity.

Lymph Nodes (Pelvis):
No pathologically enlarged external or internal iliac, obturator, or inguinal lymph nodes are seen. All visualized pelvic nodes are within normal size thresholds.

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is moderately distended and demonstrates a smooth wall without focal mass or thickening.

Musculoskeletal Structures (Pelvis):
The pelvic bony structures, including the iliac wings, sacrum, and proximal femora, are intact without destructive or sclerotic lesions. No periosteal reaction.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence. The bilateral mastectomy sites appear intact with tissue expanders in place and no suspicious chest wall or axillary nodularity. The post-operative left axillary bed demonstrates expected post-dissection changes without worrisome adenopathy.

2. The lungs are clear without pulmonary nodules, consolidation, or ground-glass opacity. Prior Keytruda-related pulmonary toxicity is not appreciable on this examination.

3. No hepatic, splenic, adrenal, or renal lesions to suggest metastatic involvement.

4. No mediastinal, hilar, retroperitoneal, or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

5. No suspicious osseous lesions in the visualized axial and appendicular skeleton.

6. The abdomen and pelvis are otherwise unremarkable without ascites, peritoneal thickening, or other findings suggestive of occult metastatic disease.

Overall, this study is reassuring with no imaging findings to suggest distant metastatic breast cancer or locoregional recurrence at this point in the patient's adjuvant treatment course.
